title: Blackrock's Crypto Trap
transcript: Wall Street's largest asset manager just put its  name on a fund that promises to bring trillions   of dollars of traditional finance onto public  blockchains. Larry Frink, the man who once   called Bitcoin an index of money laundering, now  says every stock, every bond, every fund, every   asset on the planet can and will be tokenized. The  marketing pitch is extraordinary. 24/7 liquidity,   instant settlement, fractional ownership,  and a flood of institutional capital that   will send the price of base layer tokens to the  moon. But buried inside own letter to investors   is a single sentence that changes everything.  He wrote that tokenized funds will become as   familiar to investors as ETFs provided, in his  words, quote, "we crack one critical problem,   identity verification." Now, that's not a mere  footnote. It actually does a lot of heavy lifting.   Because what Wall Street is quietly building  is not a bridge that brings traditional finance   onto crypto's terms, but a compliance architecture  designed to bring crypto onto theirs. And once the   rails are built, once the whitelists are in place,  once every major protocol bends to the pressure,   the trap is sprung and there's no going back. My  name is Guy and you're watching the Coin Bureau.   So, is the $7 trillion tokenization wave actually  the mass adoption breakthrough this industry has   been waiting for? Or is it a Trojan horse that  will gut everything crypto was built to achieve?   And what does any of this mean for the assets  you're holding right now? Well, that's exactly   what we're going to cover today. First though,  let's look at the evidence behind Wall Street's   sudden obsession with tokenization because the  numbers here are genuinely staggering. BlackRock,   the world's largest asset manager with 11.5  trillion under management, launched its Bidd4. That fund has grown to over $1.8 billion in assets  under management. JP Morgan's blockchain division,   Kexis, has now processed over $1 trillion in  transactions through its JPMCoin settlement   infrastructure. Cityroup has deployed token  services for crossber payments and published   what has become one of the most widely cited  reports in the space projecting the tokenized   asset market could reach 4 to5 trillion by 2030.  Now, the broader tokenized treasury market has   already crossed $7.3 billion, up $256% in a single  year. And the stated goal from every single one   of these institutions is efficiency, instant  settlement instead of the 2-day clearing cycle,   programmable collateral instead of static  balance sheets, and 24/7 liquidity for   assets that currently freeze on weekends and  holidays. Now, on the face of it, this all sounds   fantastic. That's fantastic. Retail investors  and crypto Twitter have been cheering this on,   operating on the assumption that $7 trillion in  traditional assets flowing onto public blockchains   is straightforwardly bullish for the underlying  networks. More utility means more demand for block   space, which means higher prices for ETH, Soul,  and every other base layer token. It's a clean,   logical narrative. The problem though is that  if you read the actual fine print of how these   tokenized assets are permitted to move, a very  different picture begins to emerge. Because while   the headline is adoption, the subtext is control.  To understand the trap, you have to understand the   technical mechanics of how these tokenized assets  actually work. Wall Street cannot legally allow   a tokenized US Treasury bill to be anonymously  traded by a pseudonmous wallet. Securities law   and AML regulations prohibit it. So, every single  one of these institutional tokenized products   is wrapped in a smart contract standard that  enforces strict KYC and AML whitelisting at the   wallet level before any transfer can execute.  The dominant standard for this is called ERC   3643. Originally developed by a Luxembourg  company called Tokeny Solutions, ERC 3643 is   built specifically to make permissioned tokens  deployable on Ethereum's permissionless base   layer. Here's how it works. A standard ERC20  token, the kind that powers most of DeFi,   is permissionless by default. Anyone can send it,  anyone can receive it, and no contract checks your   identity first. ERC 3643 reverses that logic  entirely. Every transfer is rooted through   an onchain identity contract called onchain  ID, which checks whether both the sender and   receiver have been verified and whitelisted by a  trusted issuer. If your wallet address is not on   the approved list, the transfer fails. Full stop.  The contract rejects you automatically before a   single token moves. And the administrator of  that whitelist has even more alarming powers   built directly into the standard. They can freeze  individual addresses. They can execute forced   transfers of your tokens without your consent. And  they can even recover tokens from wallets whose   private keys have been lost. Tokeny, the company  behind ERC 3643, now powers over $32 billion in   tokenized assets using this architecture. DTCC  joined the ERC 3643 association in March 2025,   adding support to its core market infrastructure  platform. So this is not a fringe experiment,   but is rapidly becoming the standard compliance  layer for institutional tokenization globally.   Now, let's look at BlackRock's Bidd specifically  because it's the most powerful case study in how   this compliance architecture actually operates in  the real world. Bidd is a tokenized money market   fund built on Ethereum. It holds US Treasury  bills and generates yield. When you look at the   smart contract, you find a built-in white list  mapping approved investor wallet addresses. Any   attempt to transfer biddens to an address not  on that list is automatically rejected by the   contract. The company managing that white list is  Securitize which is also the entity BlackRock has   made a strategic investment in and placed on its  own board. Now Securitize is an SEC registered   broker dealer and transfer agent. They run the  KYC and AML onboarding process every participant   must complete before their wallet address is added  to the approved list. And the minimum investment   to access bidd $5 million in USDC. The minimum  redemption is $250,000. So this is not retail   defy. This is a gated institutional product that  happens to be deployed on a public blockchain.   And here's the part that should make every DeFi  enthusiast pay close attention. In February 2026,   Black Rockck announced a partnership with  Securitize to list Bidd on UniS swap X, the   leading decentralized exchange. On the surface,  this sounds like a landmark moment. A trillion   dollar asset manager bringing a regulated product  onto a permissionless protocol. Except that is not   what is actually happening. When you dig into the  mechanics, only wallet addresses that have already   passed securitizes verification process can hold  Bidd tokens. The DEX infrastructure is being   used purely as an execution layer. The compliance  layer sits above it, filtering who can participate   before any trade executes. The permissionless  protocol is essentially being rented out to   run a permissioned financial product. UniS swap's  code may be open, but the market it's running is   closed. And speaking of staying ahead of these  regulatory and structural developments before   they reshape your portfolio, this is exactly why  you need the Coinb Telegram channel in your life.   It's where we share the latest breaking news,  provide deep dive alpha, and give you the most   important market updates, all pinged directly to  your device so you don't miss a thing. Whether   it's an institutional compliance move that changes  the DeFi landscape or a regulatory signal that the   rest of the market hasn't priced in yet, sign up  today using the link down below or by scanning the   QR code on screen. Now, here's where the story  gets a little complicated because the bullish   case for institutional tokenization deserves to be  taken seriously before we tear it apart. At 4.05%   05% on the 10-year Treasury. There is a real yield  arbitrage case for putting US government bonds on   chain as programmable collateral. When DeFi money  markets were yielding half a percent, there was   no reason to tokenize treasuries. At 4%, there  is an actual use case. And there is one token in   the RWA space whose performance over the last 90  days tells a genuinely interesting story. Maker,   now rebranded as Sky Protocol, is up 22.8 8% in  the last 3 months, while every other RWA adjacent   token has collapsed. Ono Finance, one of the  most widely cited RWA narrative plays, is down   79% from its all-time high despite its platform  TVL growing 404% year-over-year. Think about that   divergence for a moment. The platform is growing  explosively, but the token is in freefall. The   reason is straightforward. Onondo is a governance  token for infrastructure that Wall Street uses   and Wall Street has absolutely no intention of  sharing the revenue with retail token holders.   MKR by contrast uses actual RWA derived income to  buy and burn tokens creating direct economic value   for holders. Over the past year, real world assets  generated nearly 80% of Maker Dow's fee revenue.   that revenue flows back to token holders through  a deflationary burn mechanism. The difference   between these two tokens is the difference between  being in the room where it happens versus watching   through a window while the institutions take  everything. So yes, there is a bullish case,   but it's narrow and it comes with a cost that most  of the mainstream narrative is not pricing in.   But there's a further twist in this tale because  the co-option of DeFi is not theoretical at this   point. We're already watching it happen in real  time. Ave, the world's largest decentralized   lending protocol, built a fully permissioned  version of itself called Ave Arc in January 2022.   Every user had to be whitelisted by Fireblocks, a  regulated custodian, before they could access the   liquidity pools. And critically, Fireblocks was  given a guardian role in Ave Arc's governance,   meaning a private regulated company had veto  power over governance proposals if they judged   them a compliance risk. A centralized override  in a supposedly decentralized protocol. Now,   Aveark eventually failed to attract meaningful  volume and was quietly wound down. But Ave did   not abandon the institutional direction. They  doubled down. In 2025, they launched Ave Horizon,   a new permissioned market for tokenized real  world assets that uses Chainlink's automated   compliance engine to enforce KYC and AML  rules crosschain. The marketing phrase is   revealing permissioned collateral, permissionless  liquidity. In other words, the institutions get to   decide which assets are acceptable and DeFi's  open infrastructure provides the execution   layer to make those assets more efficient. This  is the pattern playing out across the entire   ecosystem. Chainlink's cross-chain infrastructure  protocol CCIP has deployed what it calls the   automated compliance engine which enforces KYC and  AML policies as assets move between blockchains.   The partners using this system include JP Morgan,  A&Z Bank, UBS, and crucially, Chain Analysis,   the firm that governments around the world used  to track and analyze crypto transactions. Chain   Analysis is now embedded directly into Chain  Link's cross-chain compliance layer. When an   asset moves from one chain to another through  CCIP with the compliance engine enabled,   it does not just teleport. It gets screened  against regulatory policies before it arrives.   The permissionless movement of value across change  is quietly being replaced by permissioned movement   filtered through an institutional compliance  infrastructure and 60 blockchain networks are   now connected to this system. Then there's the  question of what happened to Ethereum itself under   regulatory pressure. In November 2022, following  the OFAC sanctioning of Tornado Cashache,   the percentage of OFAC compliant blocks on  the Ethereum network peaked at 80%. Eight out   of 10 blocks on the world's most important smart  contract platform were censoring transactions to   comply with US Treasury sanctions. Community push  back eventually brought that number down to around   27%. But the lesson is stark. The censorship  didn't require a protocol upgrade, and it didn't   require Ethereum's developers to do anything. It  only required a handful of private relay operators   to make a commercial and legal decision about  which transactions they would process. As one   Ethereum Foundation researcher wrote at the time,  quote, "Blockers are just people with families who   might want to move to the United States, and it's  understandable that they try to minimize the risk   for themselves and their business. But that is not  decentralization. It's centralization wearing the   mask of decentralization. And that brings us to  the bitter irony at the center of all this irony.   On the 3rd of January 2009, Satoshi Nakamoto  embedded a message into Bitcoin's genesis block.   The Times Chancellor on the brink of second  bailout for banks. It was less a timestamp   and more a declaration of intent. Bitcoin was  created to be money that no government or bank   could control. Ethereum, meanwhile, was built to  run unstoppable applications. The entire promise   of this technology was financial freedom, the  ability to transact peer-to-peer without asking   permission from gatekeepers. Eric Hughes wrote in  the Cipher Punk Manifesto in 1993 that privacy is   necessary for an open society in the electronic  age. That principle is what this entire movement   was built on. But now those same gatekeepers  are walking through the front door wearing the   mask of institutional adoption and much of the  market is cheering them on because number go up.   Jaime Diamond, who spent years calling Bitcoin  rat poison and linking it to moneyaundering, now   says JP Morgan's blockchain is private, meaning  it has, in his exact words, total control of who   uses the chain and how. And that sentence should  stop you cold because that is the explicit goal,   not efficiency, not democratization, control. The  DeFi Education Fund put it plainly in late 2025.   Satoshi didn't invent Bitcoin so we could copy  paste Wall Street gatekeepers into web 3 and   call it compliance. And yet that is precisely what  is being built. To be blunt then the $7 trillion   tokenization wave is a double-edged sword and  the edge facing retail investors is considerably   sharper than the marketing suggests. The  institutional infrastructure, efficiency gains,   and the yield case for onchain treasuries at 4%  are all real. And if you hold MKR or other tokens   with genuine revenue sharing mechanics tied to  RWA adoption, well, there is a case that this   trend works for you. But Wall Street's version  of DeFi is not DeFi. It's permissioned finance   running on public rails where every participant  is identified, every transaction is screened   and the administrators of the white list have the  technical power to freeze your assets and reverse   your transfers. That is a feature, not a bug. The  endgame of the RWA narrative is not to liberate   traditional finance using crypto. It's to absorb  crypto into the traditional finance surveillance   state. And the mechanism is not regulation passing  through Congress. It's institutional liquidity   flooding into protocols that bend to compliance  demands, slowly reorienting the norms of what DeFi   is supposed to be until the original version  is not destroyed, but simply forgotten. Now,   Vitalic Peterin published a new proposal just days  ago aimed at ensuring transactions cannot be fully   censored from Ethereum's base layer, regardless  of how centralized block building becomes. That   fight is worth watching closely. and truly  decentralized protocols with no central entity   remain outside the scope of current regulatory  frameworks. Which is exactly why self-custody   and genuinely permissionless infrastructure matter  more now than they ever have. The question is not   whether tokenization will happen. The data makes  it clear it already is. The question is whether   we will recognize the crypto ecosystem on the  other side of it. Because once those compliance   rails are built, once the whitelists are in place,  once every major protocol has a guardian with veto   power, the architecture of financial control is  not something you simply switch off. Bitcoin was   created so that no chancellor would ever need to  bail out a bank using money printed without your   consent. We should now at least have the honesty  to ask whether we're actually building something   different. But what do you think? Is the RWA  tokenization trend the mass adoption breakthrough   we've been waiting for? Or is it the Trojan horse  that will gut everything crypto was built to   achieve? Let me know down in the comments because  this debate is going to define the next decade of   this industry. And if you want to understand how  governments are using tax laws as yet another tool   to control your crypto holdings, then check out  our video on that right over here. All right,   folks. Thank you all for watching and I'll  see you again soon. This is Guy signing off.
summary_hu: A BlackRock által indított tokenizált alapok körüli izgalom mögött a decentralizált finanszírozás iránti intézményi érdeklődés áll. A tokenizálás előnyei között szerepel a 24/7-es likviditás és az azonnali elszámolás, azonban a megvalósítás szigorú KYC és AML ellenőrzéseken alapul, ami korlátozza a felhasználók anonimitását. A Wall Street beépíti a kontrollt a decentralizált pénzügyi rendszerekbe, eltérítve a kriptovaluta eredeti célját: a közvetlen tranzakciót bankok és kormányok nélkül. A hagyományos pénzügyi piacok technikai mechanizmusokkal való összekapcsolása kockázatot jelent a kriptovaluták jövőjére nézve. Emellett a hagyományos eszközök tokenizálása új lehetőségeket is mozgósíthat, de a retail befektetők számára ez egy éles penge kétélű kardot jelent.
summary_en: The excitement surrounding BlackRock's launched tokenized funds highlights institutional interest in decentralized finance. The advantages of tokenization include 24/7 liquidity and instant settlement, but their implementation relies on strict KYC and AML verifications, curtailing user anonymity. Wall Street is embedding control within decentralized financial systems, deviating from the original purpose of cryptocurrency: peer-to-peer transactions without banks and governments. The coupling of traditional market mechanisms with technology poses risks for the future of crypto. Additionally, the tokenization of traditional assets might mobilize new opportunities, but for retail investors, this presents a sharply dual-edged sword.
key_points_hu: A BlackRock tokenizációs hullám a decentralizált pénzügyek irányába mutat, de erős korlátokkal jár.
A tokenizált eszközök nem anonim tranzakciókat tesznek lehetővé, ami ellentmond a kriptovaluták alapelveinek.
A hagyományos pénzügyi intézmények integrálása a kriptovaluták világába potenciálisan kontrollt teremt.
key_points_en: BlackRock's tokenization wave points toward decentralized finance but comes with strong limitations.
Tokenized assets do not allow for anonymous transactions, contradicting cryptocurrency principles.
The integration of traditional financial institutions into the world of cryptocurrencies potentially creates control.
